title: Foundry Agents + MCP Integration
summary: Master Microsoft Foundry Agent Service as a managed model-plus-tools runtime, then attach remote MCP servers safely using the current Azure AI Projects agent surface, managed identity, least-privilege tool allow lists, approval flows, state objects, tracing, and a complete runnable Python pattern.

concepts.title: What Foundry Agent Service Is
concepts.firstPrinciples: First principles: a model predicts the next useful text, but a real assistant must remember a task, follow standing instructions, choose tools, authenticate safely, and continue across steps. Foundry Agent Service adds a managed runtime around a deployed model so the model can operate as an application component instead of a one-shot text function.
concepts.definition: Microsoft Foundry Agent Service is the managed platform for building, deploying, scaling, and observing AI agents. An agent combines a Foundry model, instructions, tools, and state so it can reason, call tools, and complete multi-step work.
concepts.analogy: Calling a model directly is like asking a smart officer one question over a radio. An agent is like assigning that officer a mission binder, approved equipment, a logbook, and a command channel that stays open until the mission is complete.
concepts.mentalModel: Think model plus policy plus tools plus memory. The model decides; instructions constrain; tools act; state remembers; tracing explains what happened later.
concepts.diagram:
graph TD
User[User] --> Agent[Foundry agent]
Agent --> Model[Deployed model]
Agent --> Instructions[Instructions]
Agent --> Tools[Tools]
Agent --> State[Conversation state]
Agent --> Trace[Telemetry]

code.caption: Create a prompt agent using the current Azure AI Projects 2.x shape
code.content:
# pip install azure-ai-projects>=2.0.0 azure-identity
# Uses DefaultAzureCredential so local development can use az login and Azure hosts can use managed identity.
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.projects import AIProjectClient
from azure.ai.projects.models import PromptAgentDefinition

project = AIProjectClient(
    endpoint=os.environ["PROJECT_ENDPOINT"],
    credential=DefaultAzureCredential(),
)

agent = project.agents.create_version(
    agent_name=os.environ.get("AGENT_NAME", "foundry-agent-basics"),
    definition=PromptAgentDefinition(
        model=os.environ.get("MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME", "gpt-5-mini"),
        instructions="Explain Azure AI concepts with short examples and ask one check question.",
    ),
)
print(agent.name, agent.version, agent.id)

drills.q: What turns a model call into an agent?
drills.a: Persistent instructions, state, tools, identity, lifecycle, and observability around the model.

drills.q: Why not call the model directly for everything?
drills.a: Direct calls are simpler, but they do not provide the same managed tool orchestration, state, publishing, identity, and tracing.

drills.q: What are the three core components Microsoft lists for an agent?
drills.a: Model, instructions, and tools.

concepts.teachBack: An agent is a managed AI application shell around a deployed model. It remembers a conversation, follows standing instructions, and invokes approved tools. Use direct model calls for one-step text generation and agents for governed multi-step work.

glossary.definition: A model-backed application unit with instructions, tools, and state.
A configuration-defined agent that Foundry runs without your own runtime code.
A preview code-based agent hosted by Foundry in managed compute.

sources.title: Microsoft Learn: What is Microsoft Foundry Agent Service

concepts.title: Current Python SDK Surface
concepts.firstPrinciples: First principles: SDK names are contracts between your code and a cloud API. When a service evolves, the safest engineer separates current GA paths from older or preview paths instead of mixing snippets blindly.
concepts.definition: Current Microsoft Foundry quickstarts use azure-ai-projects version 2.x, AIProjectClient, project.agents.create_version, PromptAgentDefinition, project.get_openai_client, conversations, and responses with agent_reference. The older classic persistent-agent flow uses azure-ai-agents objects such as agents_client.create_agent, threads, messages, runs, and run_steps and is still visible in SDK samples.
concepts.analogy: Imagine two radio manuals on the desk: the new manual says use the digital mission console, while the old manual says operate individual channel switches. Both describe agents, but they are not interchangeable procedures.
concepts.mentalModel: For new Foundry projects, start with azure-ai-projects >= 2.0.0. Use azure-ai-agents samples only when you intentionally need the classic or persistent thread-run API shape shown by that package. Treat MCP examples as moving quickly because docs and SDK samples show both the new Responses based prompt-agent flow and the create_agent thread-run flow.
concepts.diagram:
graph TD
New[Azure AI Projects 2.x] --> CreateVersion[project agents create version]
CreateVersion --> Responses[OpenAI responses with agent reference]
Classic[Azure AI Agents samples] --> CreateAgent[agents client create agent]
CreateAgent --> ThreadRun[threads messages runs run steps]

code.caption: Print the installed packages and fail fast on mixed assumptions

drills.q: Which package does the current Foundry quickstart tell Python learners to install?
drills.a: azure-ai-projects>=2.0.0 plus azure-identity.

drills.q: What method creates a prompt-agent version in the current quickstart?
drills.a: project.agents.create_version with PromptAgentDefinition.

drills.q: Why is this concept marked uncertain?
drills.a: Because Microsoft documentation and SDK samples expose both new Projects 2.x Responses APIs and azure-ai-agents thread-run samples during a transition.

concepts.teachBack: Use the new Projects 2.x quickstart as the default path. Know that azure-ai-agents still documents thread, message, run, and run-step flows used by many samples. When copying code, match package version, endpoint format, and API family before debugging anything else.

glossary.definition: Python client for a Foundry project endpoint.
Current Projects 2.x model for defining a prompt agent.
Responses API extra body value that tells Foundry which agent to use.

concepts.title: Agent State Objects
concepts.firstPrinciples: First principles: long work needs a ledger. The user request, the agent decisions, tool calls, results, and final answer must be represented as durable objects so clients can retry, inspect, and audit work.
concepts.definition: In the thread-run model, an Agent is the configured worker, a Thread is the conversation workspace, a Message is a user or assistant entry, a Run is one execution of an agent over a thread, and RunSteps are the detailed actions taken inside that run.
concepts.analogy: A thread is a case file, messages are pages added to the file, a run is assigning the case to the agent, and run steps are the timestamped actions in the case log.
concepts.mentalModel: The object model separates state from execution. You can add messages to a thread, start a run, poll until the run is complete or needs action, then list messages and run steps to understand the result.
concepts.diagram:
graph TD
Agent[Agent config] --> Run[Run]
Thread[Thread state] --> Run
Message[User message] --> Thread
Run --> StepA[Run step model call]
Run --> StepB[Run step tool call]
Run --> StepC[Run step final message]

code.caption: Minimal thread run loop using the azure-ai-agents sample surface
code.content:
# pip install azure-ai-projects azure-ai-agents>=1.2.0b3 azure-identity --pre
# This shape mirrors Azure SDK samples that expose threads, messages, runs, and run steps.
import os, time
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.projects import AIProjectClient

project_client = AIProjectClient(
    endpoint=os.environ["PROJECT_ENDPOINT"],
    credential=DefaultAzureCredential(),
)

with project_client:
    agents = project_client.agents
    agent = agents.create_agent(
        model=os.environ["MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME"],
        name="state-objects-demo",
        instructions="Answer in one paragraph and cite which tool you used if any.",
    )
    thread = agents.threads.create()
    agents.messages.create(thread_id=thread.id, role="user", content="Explain what a run step is.")
    run = agents.runs.create(thread_id=thread.id, agent_id=agent.id)

    while run.status in {"queued", "in_progress", "requires_action"}:
        time.sleep(1)
        run = agents.runs.get(thread_id=thread.id, run_id=run.id)

    for step in agents.run_steps.list(thread_id=thread.id, run_id=run.id):
        print(step.get("id"), step.get("status"), step.get("type"))
    for message in agents.messages.list(thread_id=thread.id):
        print(message.role, message.text_messages[-1].text.value if message.text_messages else "")
    agents.delete_agent(agent.id)

drills.q: What object stores conversation history?
drills.a: The thread.

drills.q: What object represents one execution attempt?
drills.a: The run.

drills.q: Where do you inspect tool calls after execution?
drills.a: Run steps.

concepts.teachBack: The state model is a ledger for agent work. Messages record conversation content, runs record execution, and run steps expose how the result was produced. This is why agents are easier to audit than a pile of raw model calls.

glossary.definition: Conversation state container.
One agent execution over a thread.
A granular action inside a run, such as a model step or tool call.

sources.title: Azure SDK for Python: MCP create and process sample
Microsoft Learn: Foundry quickstart with Azure AI Projects 2.x

concepts.title: The Run Loop
concepts.firstPrinciples: First principles: tool-using agents are not a single request-response. The model may need to pause, ask for a tool, wait for approval, receive the result, and only then answer. The client must drive or monitor that state machine safely.
concepts.definition: The run loop is the control flow that creates or reuses conversation state, adds user input, starts a run or response, waits for completion, handles required tool actions such as MCP approvals, and reads the final messages or output text.
concepts.analogy: It is like air traffic control: clear the plane for takeoff, watch status, approve special maneuvers, and log the landing before closing the flight.
concepts.mentalModel: Never assume the first response is final. Check status or output items. If the agent asks for approval or tool output, provide it, then continue the same run or previous response ID.
concepts.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
participant User as User
participant App as Client app
participant Agent as Foundry agent
participant MCP as MCP server
User->>App: ask question
App->>Agent: create thread or conversation
App->>Agent: add message and start run
Agent->>Agent: reason over instructions and state
Agent-->>App: approval needed for tool
App->>App: review tool name and arguments
App->>Agent: approve or deny
Agent->>MCP: call allowed tool
MCP-->>Agent: tool result
Agent-->>App: final answer
App-->>User: show answer and trace id

code.caption: Responses API run loop with MCP approval items
code.content:
# pip install azure-ai-projects>=2.0.0 azure-identity openai
# Uses the current prompt-agent Responses pattern from Microsoft Learn.
import json, os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.projects import AIProjectClient
from openai.types.responses.response_input_param import McpApprovalResponse

project = AIProjectClient(endpoint=os.environ["PROJECT_ENDPOINT"], credential=DefaultAzureCredential())
openai = project.get_openai_client()
conversation = openai.conversations.create()

response = openai.responses.create(
    conversation=conversation.id,
    input="Use the attached MCP server to answer with one cited fact.",
    extra_body={"agent_reference": {"name": os.environ["AGENT_NAME"], "type": "agent_reference"}},
)

approvals = []
for item in response.output:
    if item.type == "mcp_approval_request":
        print("Review MCP call", item.server_label, getattr(item, "name", "unknown"))
        print(json.dumps(getattr(item, "arguments", {}), indent=2, default=str))
        # Production policy belongs here. This demo approves only read style tools by name.
        tool_name = getattr(item, "name", "")
        approve = tool_name.startswith(("get_", "list_", "search_", "read_"))
        approvals.append(McpApprovalResponse(type="mcp_approval_response", approval_request_id=item.id, approve=approve))

if approvals:
    response = openai.responses.create(
        previous_response_id=response.id,
        input=approvals,
        extra_body={"agent_reference": {"name": os.environ["AGENT_NAME"], "type": "agent_reference"}},
    )

print(response.output_text)

drills.q: Why can a run pause?
drills.a: The agent may require tool output, MCP approval, OAuth consent, or other action before it can finish.

drills.q: What should the client review before approving an MCP call?
drills.a: Server label, tool name, arguments, risk, and whether the tool is in policy.

drills.q: What ties a follow-up approval to the paused work?
drills.a: The previous response ID or run ID, depending on API family.

concepts.teachBack: A tool-using agent is a state machine, not a plain chat completion. Your app must watch for required action, safely answer it, and resume the same work item. The approval point is a security control, not a nuisance.

glossary.definition: A paused state where the agent needs external input such as approval or tool output.
A client response that allows or denies a requested MCP call.

sources.title: Microsoft Learn: Connect to MCP server endpoints for agents

concepts.title: Agent Tool Types
concepts.firstPrinciples: First principles: a language model cannot know fresh private data or modify systems by itself. Tools are controlled capabilities that let the agent observe or act outside the model context window.
concepts.definition: Foundry Agent Service supports built-in tools such as Code Interpreter, File Search with vector stores, Function tools executed by your code, web search and Bing grounding, plus custom tools such as MCP servers, OpenAPI tools, Azure Functions, and agent-to-agent connections. This lane focuses on MCP as the generic bridge to any MCP-compatible server.
concepts.analogy: Tools are like mission equipment in an armory. A medic kit, drone, map database, and radio each solve a different job, and the commander decides which equipment the mission is allowed to carry.
concepts.mentalModel: Pick the smallest tool that fits. Use Code Interpreter for sandboxed Python, File Search for vector-grounded documents, Function tools for your app code, Bing or web grounding for current public information, OpenAPI or Azure Functions for HTTP and serverless APIs, and MCP when a team wants to expose a reusable multi-tool server to many clients.
concepts.diagram:
graph TD
Agent[Agent] --> BuiltIn[Built in tools]
BuiltIn --> Code[Code interpreter]
BuiltIn --> Files[File search]
BuiltIn --> Web[Web search]
BuiltIn --> Bing[Bing grounding]
Agent --> Custom[Custom tools]
Custom --> Function[Function tools]
Custom --> OpenAPI[OpenAPI tools]
Custom --> Functions[Azure Functions]
Custom --> MCP[MCP servers]

code.caption: Create a web-search agent to contrast built-in tools with MCP
code.content:
# pip install azure-ai-projects>=2.0.0 azure-identity
# Built-in tools need less plumbing because Foundry hosts the execution path.
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.projects import AIProjectClient
from azure.ai.projects.models import PromptAgentDefinition, WebSearchTool

project = AIProjectClient(endpoint=os.environ["PROJECT_ENDPOINT"], credential=DefaultAzureCredential())
agent = project.agents.create_version(
    agent_name="tool-catalog-web-search",
    definition=PromptAgentDefinition(
        model=os.environ.get("MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME", "gpt-5-mini"),
        instructions="Use web search only when current public information is required.",
        tools=[WebSearchTool()],
    ),
)
print("Created", agent.name, agent.version)

drills.q: Which tool is best for private document grounding?
drills.a: File Search with vector stores.

drills.q: Which tool is best when your own Python code must execute in your app?
drills.a: Function tools if your app executes the function, or Code Interpreter if the agent needs sandboxed analysis.

drills.q: Why choose MCP?
drills.a: It packages external tools behind a reusable standard endpoint that many agents or clients can consume.

concepts.teachBack: Tools are the agent's controlled senses and hands. Choose built-ins when Foundry already provides the capability. Choose MCP when you need a reusable external tool server rather than one-off app functions.

glossary.definition: Built-in sandboxed Python execution tool.
Tool for retrieving from uploaded files or vector stores.
Agent tool that connects to a remote Model Context Protocol server endpoint.
Tooling that grounds an agent in current public web results through Bing powered capabilities.

sources.title: Microsoft Learn: Agent tools overview
Microsoft Learn: What is Microsoft Foundry Agent Service

concepts.title: Attaching Any MCP Server
concepts.firstPrinciples: First principles: an agent should not be handed the internet with unlimited powers. It needs a named server endpoint, a narrow allow list, an authentication plan, and an approval policy before model reasoning can become external action.
concepts.definition: The documented MCP tool shape includes a server label, server URL, optional allowed tools list, approval requirements, and authentication through project connection or headers. Current docs show MCPTool in azure.ai.projects.models for prompt agents and McpTool in azure.ai.agents.models for thread-run samples.
concepts.analogy: Attaching an MCP server is like adding a named radio channel to the mission plan, then listing which commands may be sent over that channel and who must approve them.
concepts.mentalModel: Use server_label as the stable human-readable name, server_url as the remote endpoint, allowed_tools as least privilege, require_approval for risky calls, and identity-backed headers or project connections for authentication. Do not give the agent every tool on a server just because discovery makes it easy.
concepts.diagram:
graph TD
Agent[Foundry agent] --> Tool[MCP tool definition]
Tool --> Label[Server label]
Tool --> Url[Server URL]
Tool --> Allow[Allowed tools]
Tool --> Approval[Approval policy]
Tool --> Auth[Managed identity auth]
Url --> Server[MCP server]

code.caption: Attach a remote MCP server to a prompt agent
code.content:
# pip install azure-ai-projects>=2.0.0 azure-identity
# This follows the Microsoft Learn prompt-agent MCP shape.
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.projects import AIProjectClient
from azure.ai.projects.models import PromptAgentDefinition, MCPTool

project = AIProjectClient(endpoint=os.environ["PROJECT_ENDPOINT"], credential=DefaultAzureCredential())

mcp_tool = MCPTool(
    server_label=os.environ.get("MCP_SERVER_LABEL", "docs"),
    server_url=os.environ["MCP_SERVER_URL"],
    # Empty or omitted can expose every server tool. Prefer a narrow allow list.
    allowed_tools=[name.strip() for name in os.environ.get("MCP_ALLOWED_TOOLS", "search_docs").split(",")],
    require_approval="always",
    # If you configured a managed-identity or OAuth project connection in Foundry, set its name or id here.
    project_connection_id=os.environ.get("MCP_PROJECT_CONNECTION_ID"),
)

agent = project.agents.create_version(
    agent_name=os.environ.get("AGENT_NAME", "mcp-attached-agent"),
    definition=PromptAgentDefinition(
        model=os.environ.get("MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME", "gpt-5-mini"),
        instructions="Use MCP tools only when needed. Explain which external source was used.",
        tools=[mcp_tool],
    ),
)
print(agent.name, agent.version)

drills.q: What does server_label do?
drills.a: It names the MCP server in tool calls and approval requests.

drills.q: Why set allowed_tools?
drills.a: It limits the agent to a deliberate subset of server tools.

drills.q: When should require_approval be always?
drills.a: For high-risk, write, delete, privileged, or poorly understood tools.

concepts.teachBack: An MCP attachment is not just a URL. It is a contract that names the server, restricts tools, authenticates safely, and defines approval. If you cannot explain each of those four parts, the agent is over-permissioned.

glossary.definition: Short label identifying one MCP server to the agent and approval UI.
Allow list of MCP tool names the agent may call.
Policy that decides whether MCP tool calls require human or application approval.

sources.title: Microsoft Learn: Connect to MCP server endpoints for agents
Azure SDK for Python: MCP create and process sample
Azure SDK for Python: azure-ai-agents models patch

concepts.title: Approval and Human Control
concepts.firstPrinciples: First principles: model intent is not authorization. Before an agent writes data, reads sensitive scopes, or calls an unfamiliar endpoint, a deterministic human or policy gate should decide whether the action is allowed.
concepts.definition: MCP approval flow pauses execution and emits an approval request containing the server, tool name, and arguments. The client reviews the request and returns an approval or denial so the agent can continue or safely stop.
concepts.analogy: It is like a weapons release protocol: the pilot can recommend the action, but command checks target, rules of engagement, and collateral risk before authorizing it.
concepts.mentalModel: Approval is a policy hook. Start strict, log every request, auto-approve only low-risk read tools with bounded arguments, and route write operations to an explicit user approval UX.
concepts.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
participant Agent as Agent
participant App as App policy
participant Human as Reviewer
participant MCP as MCP server
Agent-->>App: approval request
App-->>Human: show server tool args
Human-->>App: approve or deny
App-->>Agent: approval response
Agent->>MCP: call tool if approved
MCP-->>Agent: result

code.caption: Policy based approval handler for azure-ai-agents MCP runs

drills.q: Is model confidence the same as authorization?
drills.a: No. Authorization must come from identity, RBAC, allow lists, and approval policy.

drills.q: What should be logged for an approval?
drills.a: Run or response ID, server, tool name, arguments, decision, approver, and timestamp.

drills.q: What calls are safest to auto-approve?
drills.a: Narrow, read-only calls with bounded inputs and no sensitive data expansion.

concepts.teachBack: Human-in-the-loop is where agent autonomy meets accountability. The model proposes a tool call, but your policy approves it. Treat every approval as an auditable security decision.

glossary.definition: A control where a person or deterministic policy reviews an agent action before execution.
SDK object that approves or denies a requested MCP tool call.

sources.title: Microsoft Learn: Connect to MCP server endpoints for agents
Azure SDK for Python: MCP create and process sample

concepts.title: Managed Identity End to End
concepts.firstPrinciples: First principles: secrets are liabilities. A secure Azure agent should prove who it is to Entra ID, receive short-lived tokens, and be granted only the roles needed for the exact downstream resource.
concepts.definition: Foundry supports agent identities, project managed identity, and Microsoft Entra authentication for tools. Developers authenticate the app to Foundry with DefaultAzureCredential or ManagedIdentityCredential, and Agent Service can use an agent identity or project managed identity to authenticate tool calls to MCP servers and underlying Azure resources.
concepts.analogy: Managed identity is a military ID badge minted by the base, not a password written on paper. The badge is checked at every gate, expires automatically, and only opens assigned doors.
concepts.mentalModel: There are two identity hops. First, your client authenticates to Foundry. Second, the agent or project identity authenticates from Foundry to MCP or a backend service. RBAC must be assigned to the identity that actually reaches the resource, not merely to the developer.
concepts.diagram:
graph TD
App[Client app] --> DAC[DefaultAzureCredential]
DAC --> Foundry[Foundry project endpoint]
Foundry --> AgentIdentity[Agent identity]
Foundry --> ProjectMI[Project managed identity]
AgentIdentity --> MCP[MCP server]
ProjectMI --> MCP
MCP --> AzureResource[Azure resource with RBAC]

code.caption: Acquire a managed-identity token for a custom MCP server audience
code.content:
# pip install azure-identity
# Use this only when your MCP server expects a bearer token from this app or managed identity.
# Prefer Foundry project connections or agent identity auth when the MCP tool configuration supports it.
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential, ManagedIdentityCredential

audience = os.environ["MCP_SERVER_AUDIENCE"]  # Example: api://your-mcp-app-id or https://storage.azure.com
credential = (
    ManagedIdentityCredential(client_id=os.environ["AZURE_CLIENT_ID"])
    if os.environ.get("AZURE_CLIENT_ID")
    else DefaultAzureCredential()
)

token = credential.get_token(f"{audience}/.default")
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token.token}"}
print("Prepared Authorization header for audience", audience)
# Pass headers through the MCP tool only if the documented SDK path you use supports runtime headers.

drills.q: Why are keys excluded in this lane?
drills.a: Managed identity avoids stored secrets, enables token rotation, and ties access to Azure RBAC.

drills.q: Who needs RBAC on the backend resource?
drills.a: The agent identity or project managed identity that the tool call uses.

drills.q: What happens if the audience is wrong?
drills.a: Token validation fails even if RBAC is correct.

concepts.teachBack: Managed identity replaces copied secrets with short-lived Entra tokens. Your app authenticates to Foundry, and the agent or project identity authenticates to tools. Always assign least-privilege RBAC to the runtime identity, not just the human developer.

glossary.definition: Azure Identity credential chain that uses managed identity on Azure and developer credentials locally.
Entra service principal representing an agent at runtime.
OAuth resource identifier the access token is issued for.

sources.title: Microsoft Learn: Agent identity concepts in Microsoft Foundry
Microsoft Learn: Set up MCP server authentication

concepts.title: Full Worked Example
concepts.firstPrinciples: First principles: mastery means wiring the full path, not memorizing definitions. The app must authenticate keylessly, create a model-backed agent, attach a least-privilege MCP server, create conversation state, handle approval, and print the final answer.
concepts.definition: This example creates a prompt agent backed by a deployed model, attaches a remote MCP server, uses managed-identity friendly credentials, sends a prompt through a conversation, handles MCP approval requests, and resumes the response.
concepts.analogy: This is the whole field exercise: choose the soldier, issue orders, attach approved equipment, open the mission log, approve a controlled tool use, and debrief the result.
concepts.mentalModel: Set PROJECT_ENDPOINT, MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME, MCP_SERVER_URL, MCP_SERVER_LABEL, MCP_ALLOWED_TOOLS, and AGENT_NAME. If your MCP server requires Entra auth, configure an Entra project connection in Foundry and pass MCP_PROJECT_CONNECTION_ID, or use a managed-identity bearer header only for servers designed for that audience.
concepts.diagram:
graph TD
User[User] --> Python[Python app]
Python --> Foundry[Foundry project]
Foundry --> Agent[Prompt agent version]
Agent --> Model[Deployed model]
Agent --> MCPTool[MCP tool]
MCPTool --> Server[Remote MCP server]
Server --> Backend[Azure backend]
Foundry --> Entra[Microsoft Entra identity]
Entra --> Server

code.caption: Complete prompt-agent plus MCP approval sample
code.content:
# pip install azure-ai-projects>=2.0.0 azure-identity openai
# Environment:
# PROJECT_ENDPOINT=https://resource.ai.azure.com/api/projects/project
# MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME=gpt-5-mini
# AGENT_NAME=academy-mcp-agent
# MCP_SERVER_URL=https://your-mcp-server.example.com/mcp
# MCP_SERVER_LABEL=academy-tools
# MCP_ALLOWED_TOOLS=search_docs,read_doc
# Optional: MCP_PROJECT_CONNECTION_ID=<Foundry connection configured for Entra or OAuth auth>

import json, os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.projects import AIProjectClient
from azure.ai.projects.models import PromptAgentDefinition, MCPTool
from openai.types.responses.response_input_param import McpApprovalResponse

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
project = AIProjectClient(endpoint=os.environ["PROJECT_ENDPOINT"], credential=credential)
openai = project.get_openai_client()

allowed_tools = [t.strip() for t in os.environ.get("MCP_ALLOWED_TOOLS", "search_docs").split(",") if t.strip()]
mcp_tool = MCPTool(
    server_label=os.environ.get("MCP_SERVER_LABEL", "academy-tools"),
    server_url=os.environ["MCP_SERVER_URL"],
    allowed_tools=allowed_tools,
    require_approval="always",
    project_connection_id=os.environ.get("MCP_PROJECT_CONNECTION_ID"),
)

agent = project.agents.create_version(
    agent_name=os.environ.get("AGENT_NAME", "academy-mcp-agent"),
    definition=PromptAgentDefinition(
        model=os.environ["MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME"],
        instructions=(
            "You are a careful enterprise assistant. Use MCP only for data you cannot know. "
            "Before final answers, summarize which tool result changed your answer."
        ),
        tools=[mcp_tool],
    ),
)

conversation = openai.conversations.create()
response = openai.responses.create(
    conversation=conversation.id,
    input="Find one relevant fact from the MCP server and explain it to a beginner.",
    extra_body={"agent_reference": {"name": agent.name, "type": "agent_reference"}},
)

approval_inputs = []
for item in response.output:
    if item.type == "mcp_approval_request":
        tool_name = getattr(item, "name", "")
        args = getattr(item, "arguments", {})
        print("MCP approval request")
        print("server", item.server_label)
        print("tool", tool_name)
        print(json.dumps(args, indent=2, default=str))
        approve = tool_name in allowed_tools and not tool_name.startswith(("delete", "write", "update"))
        approval_inputs.append(McpApprovalResponse(
            type="mcp_approval_response",
            approval_request_id=item.id,
            approve=approve,
        ))

if approval_inputs:
    response = openai.responses.create(
        previous_response_id=response.id,
        input=approval_inputs,
        extra_body={"agent_reference": {"name": agent.name, "type": "agent_reference"}},
    )

print(response.output_text)

drills.q: What six values must you know before running the sample?
drills.a: Project endpoint, model deployment, agent name, MCP server URL, server label, and allowed tool names.

drills.q: Where does keyless authentication enter the sample?
drills.a: DefaultAzureCredential authenticates the app to Foundry, and the MCP tool can use a project connection or managed identity based authentication.

drills.q: Why does the sample approve only allow-listed tools?
drills.a: Allowed tools are least privilege, and approval enforces that policy at runtime.

concepts.teachBack: The full pattern is project client, agent definition, MCP tool, conversation, response, approval, resume, final answer. Managed identity keeps credentials out of code. The allow list and approval step keep tool use intentional.

glossary.definition: The Foundry project URL used by AIProjectClient.
Foundry-managed connection object for tool authentication configuration.
Versioned server-side definition of a prompt agent.

concepts.title: Persistent vs Ephemeral Agents
concepts.firstPrinciples: First principles: some agents are products and some agents are temporary workers. Products need versioning, endpoints, identity, monitoring, and governance. Temporary workers need fast startup and cleanup.
concepts.definition: A persistent agent is created, versioned, reused, published, and monitored over time. An ephemeral agent or in-process hosted pattern is created for a session or runtime flow and can be discarded after the task.
concepts.analogy: A persistent agent is a standing unit with a call sign. An ephemeral agent is a temporary patrol assembled for one mission.
concepts.mentalModel: Use persistent prompt agents for stable business assistants, repeatable tools, and audit trails. Use ephemeral patterns for experiments, dynamic one-off orchestration, or framework-hosted agents where the code owns most behavior.
concepts.diagram:
graph TD
Need[Agent need] --> Stable[Stable repeated workflow]
Need --> Temporary[One off workflow]
Stable --> Persistent[Persistent versioned agent]
Temporary --> Ephemeral[Ephemeral runtime agent]
Persistent --> Publish[Publish and monitor]
Ephemeral --> Cleanup[Discard after session]

code.caption: Create a reusable agent name that can be promoted later
code.content:
# pip install azure-ai-projects>=2.0.0 azure-identity
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.projects import AIProjectClient
from azure.ai.projects.models import PromptAgentDefinition

project = AIProjectClient(endpoint=os.environ["PROJECT_ENDPOINT"], credential=DefaultAzureCredential())
agent_name = os.environ.get("AGENT_NAME", "stable-support-agent")

agent = project.agents.create_version(
    agent_name=agent_name,
    definition=PromptAgentDefinition(
        model=os.environ["MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME"],
        instructions="Stable support agent. Prefer approved tools and answer with traceable reasoning.",
    ),
)
print("Created reusable version", agent.name, agent.version)
# Keep persistent agents. Delete only scratch agents created for experiments.

drills.q: When is persistent better?
drills.a: When the agent is reused, governed, published, monitored, or given stable permissions.

drills.q: When is ephemeral better?
drills.a: For experiments or short-lived workflows where the runtime code owns orchestration.

drills.q: What changes after publishing?
drills.a: A production agent can receive a distinct identity and stronger lifecycle governance.

concepts.teachBack: Agent lifetime is an architecture decision. Persistent agents are governed products; ephemeral agents are disposable workers. Pick the lifetime before assigning permissions, because identity and audit needs follow lifetime.

glossary.definition: Agent definition intended to be reused and governed over time.
Short-lived agent instance or in-process flow created for a narrow task.

sources.title: Microsoft Learn: What is Microsoft Foundry Agent Service
Microsoft Learn: Agent identity concepts in Microsoft Foundry

concepts.title: Connected and Multi Agent Patterns
concepts.firstPrinciples: First principles: complex work is often a team sport. Instead of one giant agent with every permission, split roles so each agent has a small mission, small toolset, and small identity blast radius.
concepts.definition: Connected or multi-agent patterns let agents delegate to other agents or expose curated capabilities through agent-to-agent links, MCP endpoints, or Foundry Toolboxes. This lane keeps it brief: MCP can act as the shared tool boundary, while dedicated agents own specialist reasoning.
concepts.analogy: It is a command structure: the operations officer coordinates, the medic handles medical tasks, the logistics officer handles supplies, and each has only the equipment needed for that role.
concepts.mentalModel: Prefer narrow specialist agents over one all-powerful agent when permissions, audit trails, or domain instructions differ. Use MCP or Toolbox to share common tools without copying configuration everywhere.
concepts.diagram:
graph TD
Orchestrator[Coordinator agent] --> Research[Research agent]
Orchestrator --> Action[Action agent]
Research --> Toolbox[Shared MCP toolbox]
Action --> Service[Backend service]
Toolbox --> Server[MCP servers]

code.caption: Represent a simple routing table before implementing multi-agent calls

drills.q: Why split agents?
drills.a: To separate instructions, permissions, blast radius, and audit trails.

drills.q: What can MCP provide in a multi-agent design?
drills.a: A shared standard endpoint for tools used by multiple agents or frameworks.

drills.q: What is the danger of one mega-agent?
drills.a: It tends to accumulate too many tools, roles, and permissions.

concepts.teachBack: Multi-agent design is permission design. Split roles when authority differs. Use MCP or Toolbox as a shared tool plane, not as an excuse to give every agent every capability.

glossary.definition: Agent or code that routes work among specialists.
Preview Foundry bundle of tools exposed as an MCP-compatible endpoint.

concepts.title: Tracing and Observability
concepts.firstPrinciples: First principles: you cannot operate what you cannot see. Agent failures often hide in intermediate tool calls, bad arguments, slow backends, or missing context, so every step needs telemetry.
concepts.definition: Microsoft Foundry tracing captures inputs, outputs, tool usage, retries, latency, token consumption, and costs using OpenTelemetry concepts such as traces and spans, with storage and inspection through Foundry and Application Insights. Prompt-agent tracing is generally available, while some hosted and external agent tracing is preview.
concepts.analogy: Tracing is the mission recorder. It shows who asked, which route was chosen, which tools fired, how long each step took, and where the mission failed.
concepts.mentalModel: Enable tracing before production. Redact sensitive content from prompts, tool arguments, and span attributes. Use trace IDs in support tickets so engineers can jump from a bad answer to the exact model and tool sequence.
concepts.diagram:
graph TD
Request[User request] --> Trace[Trace]
Trace --> SpanA[Model span]
Trace --> SpanB[Tool call span]
Trace --> SpanC[MCP server span]
Trace --> Metrics[Latency tokens cost]
Trace --> AppInsights[Application Insights]

code.caption: Add application side OpenTelemetry span around an agent request
code.content:
# pip install opentelemetry-api
# Foundry provides platform tracing; app spans help correlate your UI or API request with Foundry work.
from opentelemetry import trace

tracer = trace.get_tracer("foundry-sdk-academy")

def ask_agent(openai, agent_name: str, question: str):
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("academy.ask_agent") as span:
        span.set_attribute("agent.name", agent_name)
        span.set_attribute("question.length", len(question))
        response = openai.responses.create(
            input=question,
            extra_body={"agent_reference": {"name": agent_name, "type": "agent_reference"}},
        )
        span.set_attribute("response.id", response.id)
        return response.output_text

drills.q: What does a span represent?
drills.a: One operation inside a trace, such as a model call or tool call.

drills.q: Why redact telemetry?
drills.a: Traces may contain prompts, outputs, tool arguments, and sensitive data.

drills.q: Where can Foundry traces be inspected?
drills.a: In the Foundry portal and Azure Monitor Application Insights when configured.

concepts.teachBack: Tracing is the black box for agent missions. It turns invisible reasoning and tool calls into inspectable spans. Production agents need tracing, redaction, and access controls from day one.

glossary.definition: End-to-end record of one request or workflow.
Single timed operation within a trace.
Open standard for telemetry signals such as traces and metrics.

sources.title: Microsoft Learn: Agent tracing in Microsoft Foundry

concepts.title: Production Checklist for MCP Agents
concepts.firstPrinciples: First principles: an agent with tools is software with authority. Before production, reduce authority, prove identity, observe behavior, and make rollback possible.
concepts.definition: A production MCP agent should use managed identity, least-privilege RBAC, allowed_tools, approval for risky actions, trusted MCP servers, private networking when needed, tracing, evaluations, and versioned deployment.
concepts.analogy: It is like pre-flight inspection: fuel, controls, comms, weapons safety, route, weather, and abort plan must all be checked before takeoff.
concepts.mentalModel: Do not ship an MCP agent just because the demo answered once. Ship only after the tool list, identities, RBAC, logging, failure handling, and user consent paths are reviewed.
concepts.diagram:
graph TD
Checklist[Production MCP agent] --> Identity[Managed identity]
Checklist --> RBAC[Least privilege RBAC]
Checklist --> Allow[Allowed tools]
Checklist --> Approval[Approval gates]
Checklist --> Network[Public or private endpoint decision]
Checklist --> Trace[Tracing and logs]
Checklist --> Version[Version and rollback]

code.caption: Executable checklist you can run in CI or deployment review
code.content:
# This check validates required environment decisions before running a deployment script.
import os, sys

required = ["PROJECT_ENDPOINT", "MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME", "MCP_SERVER_URL", "MCP_ALLOWED_TOOLS"]
missing = [name for name in required if not os.environ.get(name)]
if missing:
    sys.exit(f"Missing required settings: {', '.join(missing)}")

allowed = [t.strip() for t in os.environ["MCP_ALLOWED_TOOLS"].split(",") if t.strip()]
if not allowed:
    sys.exit("MCP_ALLOWED_TOOLS must name at least one tool.")
if any(t == "*" for t in allowed):
    sys.exit("Wildcard MCP tools are not allowed for production.")

print("Preflight passed for MCP agent deployment settings.")

drills.q: What is the biggest MCP production smell?
drills.a: A broad or wildcard tool list combined with weak or no approval.

drills.q: Why prefer trusted MCP servers?
drills.a: Tool calls may share prompt content or receive external data, so server trust and data handling matter.

drills.q: When should private endpoints be considered?
drills.a: When the MCP server or data path must not be exposed to the public internet.

concepts.teachBack: Production MCP agents are governed systems. Identity, least privilege, approval, tracing, and trusted endpoints are mandatory design elements. A passing demo is not a production readiness signal.

glossary.definition: Granting only the access required for the task.
MCP endpoint reachable through private networking instead of public internet.

quiz.q: What is the best first-principles definition of a Foundry agent?
options: A model plus instructions, tools, state, identity, and runtime lifecycle
A static prompt saved in a text file
A personal access token for a model
Only a vector database
quiz.explain: An agent wraps a model with instructions, tools, state, and managed runtime behavior.

quiz.q: Which Python package and API shape does the current Foundry quickstart emphasize for new projects?
options: azure-ai-projects >= 2.0.0 with AIProjectClient and project.agents.create_version
azure-storage-blob with BlobServiceClient
requests with a hand-written API key
azure-ai-translation-text with TranslatorCredential
quiz.explain: The current quickstart installs azure-ai-projects>=2.0.0 and creates prompt agent versions through AIProjectClient.

quiz.q: Why should allowed_tools be set on an MCP attachment?
options: To enforce least privilege by limiting which MCP server tools the agent may call
To make the model larger
To bypass authentication
To disable tracing
quiz.explain: allowed_tools narrows authority to approved tool names.

quiz.q: What should your app inspect before approving an MCP tool call?
options: Server label, tool name, arguments, risk, and policy
Only the final answer
Only token count
Only the model deployment name
quiz.explain: Approval is an authorization decision over the requested tool action.

quiz.q: In the thread-run model, what is a RunStep?
options: A detailed action within a run, such as a model or tool call step
A region where the model is deployed
An Azure subscription
A model price tier
quiz.explain: Run steps expose the internal sequence of work inside a run.

quiz.q: What is the preferred authentication style in this lane?
options: DefaultAzureCredential or ManagedIdentityCredential with Entra and RBAC
Hard-coded API keys
Client secrets stored in source code
Shared personal access tokens in prompts
quiz.explain: The lane requires managed identity only and avoids keys and secrets.

quiz.q: Why enable tracing for agent runs?
options: To inspect inputs, outputs, tool use, latency, retries, token use, and failures
To remove the need for RBAC
To make MCP unauthenticated
To hide tool calls from operators
quiz.explain: Tracing provides operational visibility for multi-step agent behavior.

quiz.q: When is a persistent agent better than an ephemeral one?
options: When it needs stable reuse, publishing, identity, monitoring, and governance
When it should disappear after one function call
When no one needs audit logs
When it has no tools
quiz.explain: Persistent agents fit stable governed workloads.
